title: Stew peas
subtitle: Jamaican kidney bean stew with salt beef and spinners
excerpt: Red kidney beans slow-cooked with salt beef, pig's tail, coconut milk, and scotch bonnet into a thick, hearty stew. Small flour dumplings called spinners are added in the final minutes.
sourceNotes: Stew peas is a classic Jamaican one-pot meal, distinct from the rice and peas side dish. The 'peas' are kidney beans; the salt beef provides deep umami and salinity; the spinners are small twisted flour dumplings that cook in the stew liquid. Traditional stew peas uses pig's tail alongside salt beef for extra body. This version uses beef brisket or beef chuck as a readily available substitute, as pig's tail is rarely stocked outside specialist Caribbean butchers. Note: salted pig's tail and salt beef should ideally be added to the master ingredient table.

content.text: Stew peas is Jamaican comfort food at its fullest. Thick, deep red, richly savoury. The coconut milk gives the broth a touch of sweetness. The spinners (small twisted dumplings) add a contrast in texture. This is a proper meal in itself, not a side dish. It takes a couple of hours of slow cooking to come together properly.

The traditional version uses salted pig's tail alongside salt beef, both soaked overnight to remove excess salt before cooking. This recipe uses beef brisket for accessibility, salted in the pot rather than pre-soaked. For the authentic result, seek out salted pig's tail and salt beef from a Caribbean butcher.

items.name: Kidney beans
items.prepNote: tinned, drained, or 300 g dried, soaked overnight

items.name: Beef brisket
items.prepNote: cut into large chunks, salt beef or pig's tail if available

content.text: Put the {{beef-brisket}} of beef brisket, {{kidney-beans}} of kidney beans, {{garlic}} of garlic, {{spring-onion}} spring onion bundle, {{thyme-fresh}} of thyme sprigs, {{allspice}} of allspice, and whole {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet into a large pot. Pour over the {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk and enough water to cover by 5 cm, about 1 litre. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a low simmer. Cover and cook for 60 minutes.

Mix the {{plain-flour}} of flour with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and enough cold water (roughly 60 to 70 ml) to make a firm, slightly dry dough. Pinch off small pieces, each about the size of a marble, and roll them between your palms into tapered cylinders about 3 cm long. Set aside.

After 60 minutes, the beans should be very soft and the broth thickening. Remove the spring onion bundle and thyme. Press a third of the beans against the side of the pot with the back of a spoon to thicken the broth. Drop in the spinners, stir gently, and simmer uncovered for a further 15 to 20 minutes until the dumplings are cooked through and the gravy is very thick. Taste for salt. Remove the scotch bonnet before serving.

items.fix: Remove the lid and increase the heat. Crush more beans against the side of the pot, this is the main thickening mechanism
items.cause: Too much water; beans not crushed to thicken

items.fix: Ensure the stew is at a gentle simmer when you add the spinners. They need actual heat to cook. Add 5 more minutes if still hard
items.cause: Dough too stiff; simmering too gentle

items.fix: Continue cooking for another 30 minutes. Brisket can take 90 to 120 minutes to fully tenderise at a low simmer
items.cause: Brisket cut too large or not cooked long enough

items.fix: If using proper salt beef or pig's tail, soak overnight in cold water and drain before use. Taste the broth before adding extra salt
items.cause: Salt beef was used without pre-soaking

content.text: Stew peas is weekday food in the Jamaican home kitchen, the kind of thing that simmers on the back of the hob for most of the afternoon. The kidney beans, loaded with the coconut milk and the salt of the meat, become almost a different food entirely from their tinned starting point. The spinners are the Jamaican one-pot dumpling: small, firm, slightly chewy, they collect the thick gravy and become the most satisfying thing in the bowl. Serve with white rice, which gives the gravy somewhere to go.
